title: Zeppole
subtitle: Italian yeasted fried dough balls dusted with icing sugar
excerpt: Simple Italian street food: soft, yeasted dough made into small balls or ring shapes and deep-fried until puffed and golden, then dusted generously with icing sugar. The dough is wet and loose, almost a batter, dropped by the spoonful directly into the oil. The result is light, irregular dough balls with a yeasty, slightly crisp exterior and a tender, airy interior. Made for the feast of San Giuseppe (19 March).
sourceNotes: Zeppole are documented in Neapolitan and southern Italian culinary literature as a traditional street food for the feast of Saint Joseph (19 March). Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, freely available at Project Gutenberg IT), includes fried dough preparations of this type. The yeasted, wet dough dropped into oil format is described in Neapolitan and Calabrian domestic cookery guides published before 1928.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Eat immediately after frying; they deflate and soften quickly. Make the batter up to the end of the first rise only, then fry to order.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried in vegetable oil at 175°C. The batter puffs and floats; turn with a spoon when the underside is golden.

glossaryTerms.term: Dropping wet yeast batter into oil
glossaryTerms.definition: Transferring portions of a very wet, batter-like yeasted dough directly into hot oil using two wet spoons or a small ice cream scoop. The batter should be too wet to shape by hand but thick enough to hold a rough ball shape when scooped. Wet the spoons in water between each scoop to prevent the batter sticking. Lower the scoop close to the oil surface before releasing; dropping from a height creates splatter. The batter will spread slightly in the oil before beginning to set and puff.

content.text: Zeppole batter is much wetter than a standard doughnut dough: it is scooped, not rolled.

attrs.term: Dropping wet yeast batter into oil

content.text: Dropping the batter directly into the oil

with wet spoons is the only shaping step. The zeppole are done when they float, have turned themselves over naturally (or been gently turned), and are golden all over. Dust with icing sugar while still hot.

Combine the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a large bowl. Add the warm water and olive oil and mix with a fork to a smooth, thick batter. Cover and leave to rise for 60 minutes until the batter is bubbly and has increased in volume.

Heat the vegetable oil to 175°C. Using two wet spoons, scoop walnut-sized portions of the batter and lower gently into the oil. Fry in batches of 6 to 8 for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until evenly golden. The zeppole may turn themselves; if not, flip gently with a spoon. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly. Dust generously with icing sugar and serve immediately.
